caveats: a: No source measures wage-weighted AI-substitutable tasks specifically for NAICS 926120.
a: The official industry combines office administration with field and safety work, so agency-level occupation mixes may differ substantially.
a: DOT evidence is federal and may not generalize to state, local, port, or parking authorities.
rho: This is bounded judgment rather than a measured implementation rate.
rho: Federal DOT adoption may proceed faster than smaller state and local agencies, while some modal regulators may have more mature data systems.
rho: The estimate excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Regulation and Administration of Transportation Programs

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The official industry is composed of government establishments funded primarily through appropriations, taxes, user fees, fines, and statutory charges rather than recurring commercial service contracts. No defensible population of private LMM firms with an in-code external-customer revenue model was identified.
lens.excluded_activities: Government departments, authorities, the Coast Guard, motor-vehicle licensing agencies, and captive public units are excluded by the private-firm control lens. Private engineering, consulting, software, testing, and transportation-operating businesses classified in other NAICS codes are also excluded.
lens.included_activities: The frozen lens would include only US lower-middle-market firms classified in NAICS 926120 that repeatedly provide transportation-program administration, regulation, licensing, planning, inspection, or investigation to external customers.

a.bridge: ILO task-level evidence covers occupations worldwide rather than NAICS 926120, while DOT evidence shows US transportation agencies adopting generative AI, natural-language processing, computer vision, and predictive analytics. The estimate maps high clerical and digitized-professional exposure to document intake, licensing, case research, report drafting, public inquiry, and analytic work, then discounts for field inspection, enforcement discretion, hearings, and safety-critical decisions. It may overstate substitution where legal accountability requires direct human review.
caveats: No source measures wage-weighted AI-substitutable tasks specifically for NAICS 926120.
The official industry combines office administration with field and safety work, so agency-level occupation mixes may differ substantially.
DOT evidence is federal and may not generalize to state, local, port, or parking authorities.
a.change_evidence: A staffing-weighted task inventory from state departments of transportation, motor-vehicle agencies, parking authorities, modal safety regulators, and the Coast Guard, paired with time studies before and after AI deployment, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Transportation-program administration contains substantial document, data, communication, and research work exposed to AI, but inspection, investigation, adjudication, enforcement, and safety judgments constrain substitution. The range represents wage-weighted current tasks, not jobs eliminated.

d5.bridge: BTS forecasts freight transportation quantity and value rather than demand for regulation and administration. The range treats sustained and growing freight movement, plus continued multimodal complexity, as workload drivers for licensing, planning, inspection, investigation, and safety administration, while allowing productivity, policy, and modal-mix changes to decouple regulatory workload from traffic. It may overstate demand if digital self-service and risk-based inspection reduce service intensity faster than transport activity grows.
caveats: Freight is only one portion of the industry's service basket.
The BTS forecast horizon and quantity differ from a five-year constant-quality regulatory-demand ratio.
Political priorities, fee policy, agency budgets, and safety mandates can dominate transport-volume effects.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-dollar workload and output forecasts from federal, state, and local transportation agencies, including licenses, inspections, investigations, planning reviews, enforcement cases, transactions, and service standards, would materially refine the ratio.
d5.rationale: Transportation activity and system complexity should sustain the current regulatory and administrative service basket. BTS projects US freight tonnage to grow substantially through 2050, supporting a modest five-year increase, but constant-quality demand need not track freight one-for-one and passenger, parking, and licensing workloads may follow different paths.

caveats: Adjacent private contractors may perform similar work but are generally classified in consulting, engineering, software, testing, or other service industries.
The dataset-supplied LMM firm count is unavailable for this government industry.
e.change_evidence: A validated establishment universe with ownership type, primary NAICS, external-customer revenue, normalized EBITDA, and evidence of recurring outsourced transportation-program work would be required to estimate eligibility.
e.rationale: The Census definition expressly describes government establishments, and no defensible denominator of LMM firms inside the frozen outsourced-service lens was found. Assigning zero would falsely convert a classification mismatch and missing firm population into an observation.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($30,826,943,515) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0000 (margins.json[92]: ESTIMATE: public administration — EBITDA is not meaningful for government units; margin 0 makes the n_band chain yield 0 by construction (recorded as a known gap)); revenue/employee $374,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at --); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: This estimate concerns operator-required quantity, not implementation feasibility or labor savings.
The frozen lens seeks private firms even though the legally accountable operators identified by the industry definition are government establishments.
Legislative changes could either expand mandatory oversight or authorize more self-service and automated decisioning.
o.change_evidence: Agency service-channel data showing end-to-end self-service rates, straight-through approvals, automated inspection coverage, appeal frequency, mandated human-review rules, and functions retired after digitization would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Most year-five quantity should still require an accountable operator because licensing, enforcement, inspections, investigations, safety oversight, hearings, and statutory decisions require institutional authority and review. Portals, automated triage, machine vision, and self-service can eliminate some handling volume, but they are more likely to change operator workflows than remove the responsible agency function.

caveats: Appropriations, taxes, licensing fees, fines, and statutory charges are not commercial customer contracts.
Contract economics from adjacent government contractors should not be imported without first resolving industry classification.
q.change_evidence: Executed contracts and renewals for a validated in-code private-firm sample, including pricing bases, cost-reduction sharing clauses, benchmarking rights, recompete outcomes, and gross-margin bridges after automation, would permit an estimate.
q.rationale: The official industry lacks an evidenced population of private recurring-service contracts under the frozen lens, so there is no defensible billing structure, renewal process, or competitive set from which to estimate retained gross benefit.

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than a measured implementation rate.
Federal DOT adoption may proceed faster than smaller state and local agencies, while some modal regulators may have more mature data systems.
The estimate excludes pricing, demand, and valuation effects.
rho.change_evidence: Agency-specific AI inventories with development stage, production usage, error and appeal rates, cycle-time savings, procurement lead times, and audited labor-hour changes would materially move implementation assumptions.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be meaningful because DOT already reports internal, research, and citizen-facing AI uses and maintains a formal AI inventory. Realization is discounted for procurement cycles, legacy systems, fragmented records, privacy, cybersecurity, explainability, union and workforce change, due process, and human accountability in safety or enforcement decisions.

caveats: Public-authority governance changes are not substitutes for private-firm transaction evidence.
Deal evidence from adjacent contractor NAICS codes would not directly measure this industry.
s5.change_evidence: If an eligible firm universe is established, PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and broker or owner interviews could identify observed control transfers, owner age, succession plans, and failed-sale rates over a five-year window.
s5.rationale: No eligible private-firm population was established, so a five-year qualifying control-transfer probability has no defensible denominator. Government reorganizations, elections, concessions, and procurement awards are not qualifying control transfers of operating firms.

principal_driver: Document-heavy administration paired with durable statutory transportation oversight supports meaningful workflow automation and avoided hiring.
principal_weakness: The official industry is composed of government establishments, leaving no evidenced transferable LMM private-firm population under the frozen lens.
